mappings.rationale: Mineral identification and distribution (A.1) are the starting point for extraction, encompassing metal ores, salt, stone, sulfur, and phosphates (B.1.b).
Geological mapping reveals where coal, petroleum, and mineral deposits lie (A.1), making this foundational for all extraction planning.
Quarrying (B.1) and the processing of stone, sand, clay, and gravel (C.5) depend on understanding rock types and their mechanical properties.
Glass, cement, ceramics, and bricks (C.5) as well as metal processing (C.3) are materials-science problems explored in this volume.
Individual metals—aluminum, copper, gold, iron—each have distinct production and processing requirements (C.3), while air processing yields industrial gases (C.7).
Chemical processing principles underpin petroleum refining (C.1) and the conversion of coal into coke, gas, and chemicals (C.2).
Petroleum refining (C.1) and the production of petrochemicals from coal tar and oil (C.2) are applications of organic chemistry at industrial scale.
Uranium processing (C.3.s) and the nuclear fuel cycle depend on isotopic separation, connecting nuclear materials science to resource extraction.
Resource conservation (A.2.a) and sustainable management practices (A.2) are essential counterweights to extractive industry pressures.
Timber, pulp, roundwood, sawn wood, veneer, and plywood (C.8.a-b) are major plant-derived products of the extraction and processing industries.
Synthetic gemstones and industrial crystals (C.4) are manufactured using crystallographic principles that govern structure and purity.
The Industrial Revolution transformed extraction industries (B), as coal mining and iron production scaled up to feed factory systems.
Processing water to obtain salt, magnesium, oxygen, hydrogen, and other dissolved elements (C.6) is both a chemical and an industrial challenge.
Conservation of natural resources (A.2.a), particularly biological ones, constrains and guides how extraction industries operate.
Resource management (A.2) viewed through an economic lens—pricing externalities, pollution costs, and depletion—shapes extraction policy.
Fossil fuel extraction—petroleum and natural gas (B.2), coal (B.1.b)—drives climate change, making this directly relevant to A.2 (management of resources).
Petroleum and natural gas production (B.2) and coal utilization (C.2) are the energy raw materials that power modern economies.
